29.1.label: Question 29.1
29.1.scenario: A health insurance company has an established portfolio of income protection business.

(i).question: State the reasons why the insurer would want to monitor its experience.

Experience will be monitored to:          monitor actual compared to expected experience and take corrective actions as needed
         provide management information to aid business decisions                                     [½]
         make more informed decisions about pricing and about the adequacy of reserves.   [½]

(ii).question:
List the items, both in relation to the whole portfolio and per‐policy, that the company
would wish to monitor on a regular basis for this business.                              [3]

29.10.label: Question 29.10
29.10.scenario: State the reasons why a health and care insurer would analyse:

(a).question: the surplus arising over a year on its supervisory basis

Risks: The company’s aims will be to stay solvent, and subject to that, almost certainly to maximise
profits. What are the risks that might affect these aims? The risks here are the various areas
covered in part 3.1 of the Syllabus. The main risks (and the ones covered below) are mortality and
Mortality and morbidity: Fundamentally, the risk is that of actual mortality / morbidity exceeding that priced for. This risk
can be broken down into the three components of model risk, parameter risk and random
fluctuation.
There is the model risk that the expectations of mortality / morbidity are not correctly modelled –
for example, they might be modelled as constants when they will in fact vary over time.
Furthermore, the model might omit certain claim types, for example, new types of cancer that are
not currently known about, but would be covered under the policies.
There is the parameter risk that the estimates of mortality / morbidity, and hence the parameters
that define the modelled versions of mortality / morbidity, will not be correct.
To estimate mortality and morbidity, the insurance company will need to look at its experience
with its current accelerated CI contracts (assuming it has some), and possibly also related
contracts, eg stand‐alone CI insurance. However, the policyholders eligible for this new product
may be significantly different from those in the insurance company’s existing portfolio, or those
underlying any other statistics that the insurance company may look at (industry data, reinsurers).
Mortality and morbidity rates are likely to change over time. This might be a gradual change,
eg standard mortality improvements, or a one‐off change, eg a change in critical illness incidence
rates following advances in diagnosis. There is a risk that such trends / changes are not correctly
allowed for.
If mortality and/or morbidity is overestimated, then the product will be over‐priced and there will
be a risk of not selling the product. If mortality and/or morbidity is underestimated, then the
product will be under‐priced and there will be a risk of making a loss, or eventually facing solvency
problems if the new product becomes a major part of the portfolio. Of the two, underestimating
will be more problematic than overestimating.
Associated with the parameter risk is the risk of anti‐selection if the insurance company does not
price by splitting policyholders into reasonably homogeneous groups with respect to mortality
and morbidity.
There is also the risk of random fluctuations – even if the estimates of mortality / morbidity are
spot on, claims would be expected to fluctuate around the expected level. The extent to which
claims vary from expected will depend on the size of the portfolio.
Expenses: There is the risk that the contracts sold will not cover the associated expenses. This could come
First, the insurance company will not know the expenses involved with this contract. They should
be very similar to those for similar contracts – for example, the current range of critical illness
insurance contracts. But there will be some differences, for example, it will be necessary to
incorporate some checks that the potential policyholders are house‐owners, so the underwriting
Secondly, future expense inflation might: Thirdly, there is a risk of selling insufficient levels of the contract to recover the development
costs, or to make a sufficient contribution to the insurance company’s overheads.
Finally, the price structure might involve cross‐subsidy of (say) small policies by large policies,
otherwise small policies could prove uncompetitive. In this case, there is the risk that the
insurance company sells a greater than expected proportion of small policies, and that overall the
There are two completely different risks: First, if most of these policyholders will have mortgages, then in the event of economic recession
they may be especially hard hit, and may be even more likely than other critical illness insurance
policyholders to lapse their policies (if legislation does not oblige them to have such policies in
The extent to which this is true will depend on the average wealth of house owners with
mortgages, compared with the wealth of the remaining population of critical illness insurance
Secondly, there is the risk that a large number of existing accelerated critical illness insurance
policyholders who are house owners will be better off lapsing their current policies and taking out
new policies under this tax‐efficient format.

(b).question:
the change over a year in the present value of the expected profit from its existing
business.

Mortality and morbidity: To minimise the risk of modelling or parameterising mortality and morbidity risks incorrectly, the
insurance company will need to study the experience of its related contracts. Existing accelerated
critical illness and stand‐alone critical illness insurance should be a good starting point.
Reinsurers’ and industry statistics will also be a useful point of reference, especially for unusual
illnesses and more extreme ages.
The risk of pricing on the wrong mortality / morbidity basis could be reduced by offering the
contract as a reviewable premium product, rather than a guaranteed premium product.
The insurance company should use (at least) all of the rating factors that most of the competition
use (eg age, gender, smoker/non‐smoker, medical).
The insurance company will need to ensure that the policyholders undergo underwriting at least
as strict as that used for the policyholders underlying the mortality / morbidity data above.
Alternatively the mortality / morbidity ‘advantage’ of belonging to the socio‐economic group
‘house‐owners’ might be deemed sufficient to compensate for a weakening of the underwriting
procedures, eg a raising of the sums insured at which detailed medical evidence is required.
Quota share reinsurance could be taken out to reduce the financial impact of parameter error.
To deal with the risk of random fluctuations, the insurance company could use individual surplus
or aggregate excess of loss reinsurance. The extent of reinsurance required will depend on the
insurance company’s total portfolio of similar policies, its free assets and the extent to which
smooth financial results are desirable (eg more important for a proprietary than for a mutual
company).
An alternative to this would be to set up a claim fluctuations reserve, but this would require a
certain amount of capital.
Persistency: The insurance company could tackle the risk of low persistency on this product by setting
premiums assuming a high withdrawal basis, although the extent to which the insurance company
can do this is constrained by competition.
A commission structure to sales agents that shifts remuneration from initial to renewal
commission may make it less likely that the agents sell the product to people for whom it is not
The second problem, of lapse and re‐entry on current products, could be tackled in various ways.
One way would be to offer to reclassify eligible policies, charging an appropriate administration
fee. However, a realistic fee might be so high that it still leaves lapse and re‐entry as the
Another possibility would be to quantify what addition to benefit (or reduction in premiums)
would equate to the expected cost of administering a large number of lapses and re‐entries, and
offering some portion of such an increase as an incentive to continue with the policy in the
Expenses: The risk of underestimating expenses could be reduced by making it a reviewable premium
The risk of insufficient sales could be reduced by aggressive marketing, and a competitive pricing
{number of sales} × {expense contributio: This will be almost zero for very high and low premiums, and maximised for some middle‐of‐the‐
range premium – depending on elasticity of demand.
The risk that a cross‐subsidy of small policies by large policies combined with a low proportion of
high‐premium sales gives rise to an insufficient contribution to the insurance company’s
         setting the cross‐subsidy such that all policies at least pay their own marginal expenses
         profit testing the new business portfolio on a variety of new business mix assumptions

29.2.label: Question 29.2
29.2.question:
Explain the reasons why a health and care insurer needs to analyse its new business, and describe
   the types of analysis that would be carried out.

Solution: The insurer needs to be sure that it has sufficient capital to finance the new business strain
caused by the policies it sells.                                                                                     [½]
So it will need to monitor the volume of sales over time against targets …                                           [½]
… because the higher the volume of sales in any period, the more capital will be needed.                             [½]
It will also need to monitor the new business mix …                                                   [½]
… because some policy types will require more capital than others.                                    [½]
The volume of sales needs to be monitored so that the insurer can check that its per‐policy
expense assumptions remain correct.                                                                   [½]
This is because the insurer’s overheads are shared between all policies it sells, and the per‐policy
expense assumption is therefore a function of the assumed volume of future sales.                  [½]
As overheads are mostly staff costs, then falling sales may create a problem because the insurer
will end up with excessive staffing capacity.                                                  [½]
Conversely, too much new business may lead to inadequate capability to deal with the work
required, leading to:                                                                                 [½]
         processing errors                                                                           [¼]
         service delays                                                                              [¼]
         the consequent deterioration of public image.                                               [½]
As part of this, the insurer will need to ensure that the skills and competence of the staff are
appropriate to the business being written.                                                            [½]
This requires both the volume and the mix of business to be monitored.                                [½]
Lower sales than expected will also mean that all policies will be less profitable than expected. [½]
Low volumes of sales will reduce return on capital, unless the insurer is very active in managing its
capital provision.                                                                               [½]
Other aspects of the insurer’s pricing assumptions are also dependent upon the mix of business
within pricing risk groups, particularly where cross‐subsidies have been assumed.             [½]
Examples of this are where average premium sizes have been assumed, or where non‐identical
risk types have been charged identical premium rates.                                      [½]
The insurer needs to ensure that the (average) commission rates assumed in the pricing basis are
consistent with the commissions paid.                                                         [½]
This may not be the case where, for example, the insurer sells identical products (with identical
premium rates) through different distributions channels, but pays different commission rates to
each channel.                                                                                    [½]
Monitoring of volume and mix will also need to be made with reference to the market as a whole.
This is so that the reasons for the changes in business can be identified, enabling appropriate
remedial action to be taken.                                                                    [½]
For example, an overall fall in market share can indicate a general lack of competitiveness, either
in terms of price or design.                                                                     [½]
A rapid increase in sales relative to the market might indicate that other insurers might have
tightened up their risk classification systems, leaving this company exposed to adverse selection
… or that the insurer’s claim definitions are now lax compared with average market practice …
… or that its products may be under‐priced.                                                               [½]
[Maximum 9]

29.3.label: Question 29.3
29.3.scenario:
In a particular private medical insurance (PMI) market, most insurance companies have been
   charging different premium rates to males and females. Your company had noticed very little
difference in its claims experience between the genders in the past, and over the last year has
been selling PMI policies on identical terms to males and females. During this time you have
noticed a marked increase in sales, especially to female applicants.
29.3.question:
Discuss the possible reasons for this change in new business experience, and the possible
implications of this change for the insurer.

Possible reasons: The removal of apparent discrimination may be popular with the public (especially, it seems, with
... making the insurer’s products easier to promote and easier to sell than those of competitors.
The rise in sales to females may be because this insurer’s premium rates might now be
substantially cheaper than those typically offered to females by the rest of the market.                        [½]
The application procedure has been simplified slightly, which might make it more attractive for
intermediaries to sell this product rather than others. (This is unlikely to be sufficient reason on
Possible implications: Provided recent experience is consistent with previous years, then the rise in volume should
increase profits, ...                                                                                           [½]
... increase return on capital, ...                                                                             [½]
... and cover a bigger share of the insurer’s overhead costs.                                                   [½]
The increased volume, if continued, may possibly require the employment of extra staff, both for
policy and for claims administration.                                                         [½]
Provided economies of scale can be achieved, this should ultimately improve returns for
shareholders.                                                                                                   [½]
There may be short‐term administrative delays, which could damage the insurer’s reputation with
policyholders, intermediaries, or both.                                                     [½]
The increased volume, especially if it persists, may put a strain on the insurer’s capital resources.
This will only be a major issue if the insurer is short of free capital.                                        [½]
Additional financial reinsurance may then be necessary.                                                         [½]
There is a concern that the claims experience of the new policyholders may differ from previously.
There is a real possibility of anti‐selection for an insurer that charges aggregate rates when the
rest of the market charges differentially.                                                         [½]
The suggestion here is that competition must be charging higher rates for females …                    [½]
… and if this is a true reflection of future claims experience then this insurer could incur
significantly higher claims costs but without an increase in premium rates.                            [½]
This could occur despite the insurer’s own past equality of experience between the genders, for
example because the new product design might appeal to a different sector of the market or a
different class of policyholder ...                                                           [½]
… or because the insurer did not correctly identify the real differences between the genders in the
past due to inadequate grouping of data in its analyses.                                        [½]
The result could be significant losses for the insurer, ...                                            [½]
... which may lead the insurer to increase its premiums across all policies.                           [½]
On the other hand, the business may still be profitable (although yielding less per‐policy profit
than before), but if increased volumes more than compensate for this then the insurer may not
be unhappy with the situation, at least in the short term.                                        [½]
Changes to underwriting may be necessary to help bring the female experience back into line,
though this might be counter‐productive in terms of the costs involved.                      [½]
The insurer could go back to charging different rates to males and females.                       [½]
[Maximum 9]

29.4.label: Question 29.4
29.4.scenario:
A health insurance company is analysing the persistency experience of its unit‐linked long‐term

care insurance product.

(i).question:
List the data required for the analysis and discuss the level of detail to which the analysis
might be performed.

Data required: The relevant data would need to be available for this contract type separately from the insurance
Data would be required in a form suitable for calculating the appropriate exposed to risk.             [½]
This might take the form of regular censuses of policies in force during the investigation period, or
censuses of policies surviving to their policy anniversary each year.                             [½]
Counts of the number of withdrawals during each year of the investigation will also be needed,
subdivided by type of withdrawal (ie paid‐up, surrender, or lapse).                            [½]
Both exposure and withdrawal data could:          the duration since inception of policy (hence would need date of entry)
 gender:          the sales channel used for selling the policy, and possibly the individual sales agent
         the commission and/or other terms of rewarding sales which are given
 the frequency of premium payme:          the mode of premium payment (eg by some automatic payment method or by cash
 the choice of investment fund:          the original term of the policy.                                          [¼ each, maximum 2½]
Note that if switching between funds is allowed under a unit‐linked policy, any switch between
funds should not be counted as a simultaneous withdrawal and new entry.                        [½]
When subdividing the data for analysis, a compromise needs to be struck between the need to
identify the important factors that affect the persistency experience, and the need to have
sufficient volume of data in each cell in order to obtain estimates that are statistically credible. [½]
Several iterations of the grouping process would be inevitable. The insurance company should
begin at the highest level of subdivision and then combine groups that both appear too small and
statistically indistinguishable.                                                              [½]
Hence it is very likely that age groups would be combined (eg into 5‐year or 10‐year age groups),
and also duration groups at later durations (eg above a duration of five years).               [½]
On the other hand (assuming there are sufficient data) it would almost certainly be necessary to
subdivide data by individual years for durations of, say, five years and under, because of the likely
significant differences between their persistency rates.                                          [½]
Initially it would be advisable to analyse each time period (say each year) of the investigation
period separately.                                                                               [½]
This might be extended down to sub‐periods of a year if the volume of data permits, and if the
time effects on persistency rates are that significant.                                        [½]
The aim here would be to identify external and internal time‐related influences on the insurance
company’s persistency experience for this business.                                            [½]
[Maximum 6]

(ii).question: Describe why the current experience may not be indicative of the future.

Why current experience might not be indi: Persistency rates occur in the context of the external and internal circumstances at the time.                [½]
The external economic environment may be different, ...                                                       [½]
... for example, economic changes can affect personal disposable income, which may make a
policy less affordable and so reducing persistency rates.                                                     [½]
Inflation of care costs may outstrip the benefit to be provided by the policy, making the benefit
less attractive and so reduce the incentive to keep paying premiums.                              [½]
Alternatively, very high inflation of premiums may encourage people to withdraw.                              [½]
Changes in State long‐term care provision can be material, eg a significant enhancement in State
long‐term care provision may encourage a large number of private long‐term care policyholders
to discontinue their policies.                                                                 [½]
Other changes in legislation and political influences can affect persistency rates, ...                [½]
... for example, changes in the tax treatment of policies and the effects of government campaigns
to promote certain kinds of financial services.                                                [½]
The commercial environment may be different. If new competitor products are introduced which
appear more attractive to policyholders, then the existing business may lose its popularity. [½]
Publicity in the media can affect persistency rates, for example when industry‐wide good or bad
practice is highlighted, or some other factor is publicised that leads to increased or reduced public
confidence in the industry as a whole.                                                           [½]
Internal factors may influence future persistency rates, eg the effect of a demutualisation, a rights
issue, or particularly good or bad performance or publicity relative to competitors can be
influential.                                                                                      [½]
Future persistency rates could also differ significantly for future new business. This could be due
to any change in the insurance company’s selling strategy or to the product design, including: [½]
 the target market for sales:          the approach to selling practices (eg more disclosure, greater focus on selling to the
customers’ needs, improved training of sales staff)
 the commission structure:          the size of policies sold and their premium payment structures
         changes to the levels of any surrender penalties imposed.              [½ each, maximum 2]
With particular reference to the current experience, this may itself be quite atypical of the
insurance company’s normal level of persistency rates (for many of the reasons just listed above).
Also the current experience will necessarily represent a relatively small sample of the insurance
company’s historical experience and could be atypical simply just because of random effects. [½]

(iii).question: Describe how the results of the analysis might be used.

How the results might be used: As volumes are likely to be low, results may be combined with those from other persistency
... eg based on those of other similar products, or external industry data could be used, if
The results will help actuaries to reassess their view of the future, essentially feeding back into
The insurance company makes decisions based on the results of actuarial models. These
decisions will only be optimal if the assumptions made are actually borne out in practice.                     [½]
It is therefore vital that the actuary’s information, with regard to the insurance company’s
emerging experience, is kept as up to date as possible. The persistency investigation is an
For example, should the results of the persistency investigation lead the actuary to believe that
the assumptions are sufficiently out of line with the actual experience as to make the decisions
inappropriate, then the assumptions will be revised in line with these results.                   [½]
Hence this feedback can lead to changes in product pricing bases and reserves, and to any other
strategy which is sensitive to the persistency experience.                                    [½]
Other ways in which the persistency anal:          the extent to which the persistency experience is contributing to the insurance company’s
profit or loss may lead to changes in the product design (to make it less sensitive to
... or to the discontinuance terms offered (to reduce the financial impact of the risk)              [½]
         it can help to provide a check on the data, for example to help reconcile the policy
movements with the in‐force file over each year                                                      [½]
         it can be used to review the suitability of the company’s sales process, ...                         [½]
... eg comparing persistency rates between sales channels used could lead to a redesign of
the sales reward system (commission rates etc) to encourage better persistency         [½]
         it can provide data for industry‐wide or nationally collated persistency statistics.                 [½]
Note that the results of these investigations would only be part of the information that the
actuary has in order to determine the assumptions. Other factors, particularly those that might
cause the future to differ from the past, would need to be taken into account.                 [½]

29.5.label: Question 29.5
29.5.scenario:
A private medical insurance company is to investigate the claims experience on its individual
   business.
29.5.question: Describe how its claims experience data would be analysed.

Solution: The insurance company would analyse both the claim incidence rates and the claim amounts
experience.                                                                              [½]
 NCD level (if applicable):          number of people covered (if applicable).                           [¼ each, maximum 2]
It may be necessary to combine groups, depending on the volume of data in each cell and the
degree of similarity between cells, ...                                                     [½]
... so as to produce a credible amount of data in each (combined) cell.                            [½]
Similarly, the investigation period would be chosen to be the best compromise between volume
of data and ensuring that it is as up to date as possible.                                 [½]
A sensible definition of what constitutes a claim would need to be decided.                        [½]
Actual claim incidence rates would be calculated for each group, for each benefit/procedure class,
by dividing the number of new claims by the number of exposure years in each cell.             [1]
These would then be compared with the rates assumed in the pricing basis.                          [½]
The average claim amount per claim would be calculated for each cell, further subdivided by type
of benefit or procedure class.                                                                [½]
These would again be compared with expected amounts.                                               [½]
Further subdivisions may be possible, eg by hospital chain or individual surgeons / consultants
involved, depending on the purpose of the investigations.                                       [½]
Both claim incidence and claim amount experience would be examined for trends, ...                 [½]
... and reasons for departures from expected would need to be investigated.                        [½]
The company should determine whether trends in incidence rates are likely to continue.             [½]
Increases in past claim amounts would be compared against a standard inflation index, and in
relation to market information.                                                              [½]
[Maximum 7]

29.6.label: Question 29.6
29.6.scenario:
A health and care insurance company writes conventional income protection insurance contracts
   on guaranteed terms.

(i).question:
Describe why the insurance company would analyse its sickness experience.                             [4]

The state of the economy in the country where the insurance company is based has resulted in a
large rise in the level of unemployment.

Reasons: To update assumptions for future experience                                                        [½]
This will lead to the insurance company reviewing its assumptions for future claim inception
This would also allow the insurance company to monitor trends in experience.                       [½]
To monitor actual compared to expected experience and take corrective actions if needed                          [½]
Actions might be to review: To provide management information to aid business decisions                                                      [½]
This might include the decisions that have been made in the past and the consequences of the
To make more informed decisions about pricing and about the adequacy of reserves                                 [½]
Monitoring is a key part of the risk management process, within the actuarial control cycle. [½]

(ii).question:
Describe what effect this may have on the insurance company’s sickness experience, and
what action the insurance company may take as a result.

Rise in level of unemployment: The insurance company might expect an increased level of fraudulent claims, or more people
claiming who wouldn’t otherwise take time off sick.                                        [½]
Appropriate corrective action may be:          stricter claims control (ie checking that policyholder really is sick)                                 [½]
The insurance company will expect a lower level of claims being terminated, since there will be
less opportunity for sick people to return to work.                                             [½]
Corrective action could be stricter management of claims once in payment (eg more frequent
checks that a claim remains valid).                                                        [½]
There is likely to be lower persistency, if people cannot afford to pay the premiums.                            [½]
Withdrawals are likely to be selective since those in poor health would be less likely to lapse,
As a consequence of everything mentioned above, the insurance company should review its
The insurance company should then review:          premium rates, relative to the market (market rates may well rise)                                     [½]
         underwriting and rating of specific sub‐groups, eg by occupation                                       [½]
         policy conditions, cover given and exclusions.                                                         [½]
However this would apply only to new policies.                                                                   [½]
The company might also need to set aside higher reserves and/or solvency capital to reflect the
deterioration in expected future experience, if they are no longer sufficiently prudent.        [1]

29.7.label: Question 29.7
29.7.scenario:
An insurance company transacting all types of life and health business last reviewed its individual
   income protection insurance premium rates five years ago. The current product is a conventional
without‐profits product with guaranteed premium rates, offering a variety of deferred periods
and expiry ages up to a maximum expiry age of 65.

The benefit is payable until recovery, death or expiry of the contract, whichever is earlier. The
premium rates vary by age, gender, term to expiry, deferred period and occupational class. It has
been widely reported in the press that the industry sickness claims experience is poor. You have
been asked to analyse the claims experience and review the product design and premium rates.
29.7.question:
Describe the analysis of the claims experience you would carry out as part of this investigation, and
outline the problems you may encounter in practice.

Analysing experience: In order to analyse the claim experience, it is necessary to have a reasonable volume of stable,
consistent data, from which future experience and trends can be deduced.                         [½]
The analysis would involve the calculation of claim inception rates and claim termination rates. [½]
These would be analysed separately for: The insurance company may also choose to break this down further, eg by occupational class. [¼]
Claim inception rates are calculated as the number of people making a claim, divided by an
The average length of the claim is investigated by looking at claim termination rates, ie number of
claims terminating divided by an appropriate exposed to risk.                                   [½]
It would want to compare actual numbers of claim inceptions and terminations per group, against
those expected according to the current premium basis.                                      [½]
This would be done by multiplying the premium basis assumptions by the relevant exposed to risk
for each grouping to get expected numbers.                                                  [½]
If a multiple state model is being used it would be necessary to look at the reason for termination,
ie whether policyholder dies or recovers, to calculate mortality rates.                          [½]
The insurance company would also analyse the mortality experience of non‐claiming
policyholders in a similar way, though this is of less importance than the other variables.          [½]
The problems that might be encountered: It would be necessary to decide on the length of the period of the investigation.                    [½]
It should be a long enough period for the data to be credible, ...                                   [½]
... but not so long as to make the experience out of date, or distorted by social and economic
influences that may have changed over that time.                                               [½]
The grouping of data may also be an issue.                                                           [½]
The premiums vary by a number of factors including age, deferred period, occupational class. [½]
This means that for some groups the data will be very scanty and lack credibility. Hence there
must be some grouping of data.                                                                 [½]
However, the larger the groups, the greater the heterogeneity within them.                               [½]

29.8.label: Question 29.8
29.8.scenario:
A reinsurer analyses the income protection claims experience of the individual companies it
   reinsures.
29.8.question: List the reasons why the experience of two of its major clients might differ significantly.

They might also have different: ... eg different occupations and geographical locations                                              [½]

29.9.label: Question 29.9
29.9.scenario:
You are the actuary to a health insurance company writing critical illness insurance in a developed

country. You are considering the results of a recent investigation of the population, in the
territory the insurer is operating in, that analysed the incidence of certain critical illnesses by
socio‐economic class. Only the first incidence of each illness has been considered in the analysis.

(i).question:
Suggest how the results of such an analysis might be useful to the insurance company. [3]

The results of the analysis show that:
(a)       the incidence of heart attack in the affluent classes was significantly lower than that in the
deprived classes
(b)       the incidence of heart attack reduced over the last 20 years in all classes, but there was
less improvement for the affluent classes compared with the deprived classes
(c)       the correlation between incidence of cancer and socio‐economic class varied according to
the type of cancer.

In order to make the comparisons, the incidence rates were adjusted for age and gender.

The results of the analyses could be use:          to supplement existing data for use in pricing, reserving, financial planning etc (so that
         to assess to what extent to allow for socio‐economic factors (or proxies for them, such as
         to assess the risk to claims costs from changes in the mix of business (by socio‐economic
         to bring existing data more up‐to‐date                                                      [½]
         to assess what adjustments to make to any other population data in order to make them
more relevant to the target population that buys CI insurance, eg the target population is
likely to be the more affluent socio‐economic classes                                  [½]
         to assess trends, for use in projecting CI incidence rates                                  [½]
         to help refine underwriting criteria and procedures used.                              [½]

(ii).question:
Suggest reasons for the above results and indicate what further analysis might be
undertaken, assuming you had full access to the data, to confirm your suggestions.

Discussion of results: Note that answers to this part could be fairly subjective. Credit should be given to all reasonable,
(a)       Lower incidence of heart attack in the affluent classes
The lower incidence of heart attack in t:          taking more exercise (eg more able to afford health‐club membership)                        [½]
         beneficial occupational influences (eg a more healthy working environment)                  [½]
which would be expected in the more affluent groups.                                                  [½]
This could be due to affluent people having better access to education and knowledge about
health issues, and possibly being able to afford a better diet.                                       [½]
Analysis of results by smoking‐status and an indicator of dietary habits, such as weight or some
other lifestyle index, to assess the effect on incidence rates would be useful in confirming the
On the other hand, one might expect the incidence to be somewhat higher in the affluent group
due to those people having more frequent medical checks that may detect the incidence of a
heart attack earlier, eg ‘silent’ heart attacks.                                            [½]
Analysis of survival rates following heart attack, by socio‐economic class, could be undertaken as
these would be expected to be better for the affluent classes if the above suggestion was valid. [½]
The general reduction in the incidence of heart attack could be attributable to the reduced
smoking and better diet over the last 20 years in the general population.                             [½]
The improvement in incidence might be le:          affluent people are in any case more likely to find out that they have had a (minor) heart
attack earlier, ...                                                                     [½]
... and so advances in screening techniques would be expected to have had less influence
on the experience (of first incidence) for this group compared with the more deprived
classes                                                                               [½]
         there has been less scope for improvement in the affluent group                                     [½]
         there may have been a more dramatic change in factors affecting heart‐attack incidence
(such as diet) in the deprived classes.                                              [½]
Again, investigation into smoking prevalence rates, diet indicators and survival rates would be
useful, but one would consider trends over the last 20 years.                                   [½]
(c)       Reasons for the correlation to vary by type of cancer
Some types of cancer are very clearly smoking‐related (eg lung cancer), ...                                   [½]
... and so one would expect the classes where smoking is more prevalent, ie the deprived classes,
to have higher incidence of these.                                                             [½]
However, the reported incidence of other cancers may be more influenced by people identifying
symptoms early on and telling their doctors.                                                [½]
Arguably, those classes with access to better education and health and care would be more likely
to check for such symptoms and report them.                                                   [½]
Similarly, there may be screening programmes for some types of cancer, which would accelerate
the reported incidence.                                                                     [½]
If these programmes have been used by some classes to a greater extent than others have –
eg they may only be available in certain parts of the country –the effects on incidence rates would
be expected to vary by class.                                                                   [½]
There may be other factors (possibly unknown) affecting the incidence of certain types of
cancer.                                                                                                       [½]
For example, the incidence of breast cancer is thought to be influenced by the age at which
women have children, which can be higher on average for those in affluent classes.                            [½]
The variation by cancer type may be partly due to random variation. This would be more likely for
cancer types where data are limited.                                                           [½]
For each cancer type, results should be analysed by smoking status and any other factors that are
thought to influence the incidence of the cancer.                                              [½]
Medical knowledge may be useful in identifying such possible factors.                                         [½]
The impact of any screening programmes should be investigated. For example, a marked increase
in reported incidence following the introduction of a screening programme would give an
indication of the extent of its influence.                                                [½]
[Maximum 10]
